title: Converting Between Color Spaces
concept: Converting between color spaces means recomputing a pixel's numbers with a formula so the same visible color is described in a different, more useful way for the task at hand — like converting RGB to HSV right before filtering by color, or to YCbCr right before compressing.

exercises.prompt: A photo looks identical on screen whether it's stored as RGB or as HSV. What does that tell you about converting between color spaces?

options.text: Conversion just recomputes the same color into different numbers — it doesn't change what the color actually looks like.
Conversion always changes the color you see.
HSV and RGB can only describe totally different sets of colors.

exercises.explain: Right — conversion re-describes a color, it doesn't repaint it.

cards.title: Same photo, three different 'shapes' of numbers
cards.body: Take one photo and save its pixel data three ways: as RGB, as HSV, as YCbCr. Displayed on a screen, all three look exactly the same. Underneath, the numbers describing every pixel are completely different. Converting between color spaces is how you get from one set of numbers to another without touching the actual picture.

cards.title: Conversion is just a formula
cards.body: Converting a color space means running each pixel's numbers through a formula that recomputes them in a new representation — RGB's red/green/blue triplet becomes HSV's hue/saturation/value triplet, or YCbCr's luma/chroma triplet, and back again. The visible color doesn't change; only how it's described in numbers changes.

cards.title: Like converting Celsius to Fahrenheit
cards.body: Water freezes at the same temperature no matter which scale you use — 0°C or 32°F describe the identical cold. You convert between the scales because one is more convenient for a given situation. Color spaces work the same way: RGB, HSV, and YCbCr can describe the identical color, and you convert to whichever one makes your next step easier.

cards.title: A color-filtering pipeline in action
cards.body: A camera captures a frame in RGB (that's how sensors and screens work). Before searching for a specific color, the program converts that frame to HSV, because filtering by Hue is far more reliable than chasing RGB triplets around. Once the colored object is found, the result can be converted back to RGB to draw a box on the screen. Each conversion exists to make one specific step easier.

cards.title: Converting doesn't damage the image
cards.body: It sounds risky to keep 'transforming' an image's numbers, as if each conversion degrades the picture like a photocopy of a photocopy. Converting between RGB, HSV, and YCbCr is a reversible calculation — the same color comes back out if you convert back. (In practice, tiny rounding errors from limited number precision can creep in, but the visible picture stays essentially unchanged.)

cards.title: Same pixel, converted for the next step
cards.body: The frame reappears in a new form right before the step that needs it.

left.text:
Sensor and screen both speak RGB.
Natural format straight out of the camera.

right.text:
Recomputed just before color-filtering.
Same picture, reorganized for the task ahead.

exercises.prompt: What actually happens when a pixel is converted from RGB to HSV?

options.text: The same color is recomputed into a new set of numbers (Hue, Saturation, Value) using a formula.
The pixel's color is randomly reassigned.
The image is recompressed into a smaller file.
Nothing — RGB and HSV files are identical.

exercises.explain: Correct — it's a formula-based recomputation of the same color, not a change to the color itself.

exercises.prompt: True or false: You would typically convert an RGB frame to HSV right before trying to isolate pixels of one specific color.
exercises.explain: True — HSV's Hue channel makes color-based filtering far more reliable than working directly in RGB.

exercises.sentence: Converting color spaces uses a ___ to recompute the same color as a different set of numbers.
exercises.explain: Formula is correct — conversion is a defined calculation, not a guess.

exercises.prompt: Which description of a video-calling app's use of color-space conversion is accurate?

options.text: The camera captures frames in RGB, then the app converts to YCbCr before sending them over the network to compress the video.
The app converts every frame to a random color space each time, with no particular reason.
The app avoids all conversion because RGB is the only color space that exists.

exercises.explain: Exactly — converting to YCbCr right before transmission is exactly what enables efficient video compression.

exercises.prompt: An app captures frames from a camera in RGB, needs to track a colored object reliably in changing light, and then needs to save the annotated video efficiently. What's the most sensible sequence of conversions?

options.text: Convert RGB to HSV for the color-tracking step, then convert to YCbCr before saving/compressing the video.
Convert every frame to a random color space each time, with no particular reason.
Convert straight to YCbCr for tracking, since compression is the only thing color spaces are good for.
Convert to HSV and stay there for saving the video file, skipping YCbCr entirely.

exercises.explain: Right — each conversion targets the step that benefits from it: HSV for lighting-robust color detection, YCbCr for efficient compression.
